example: «faveo» (favor) governs the dative: faveo tibi (I favor you). Verbs of attitude/disposition take the dative of the person affected
«placeo» (please) governs the dative: hoc mihi placet (this pleases me). Mirror of English 'X is pleasing to Y'
«displiceo» (displease) governs the dative: hoc mihi displicet (this displeases me). Mirror of placeo
«pareo» (obey/be visible to) governs the dative: pareo legi (I obey the law). Common in Roman political/military contexts
«suadeo» (advise) governs the dative of person + acc/infinitive of advice: suadeo tibi hoc facere (I advise you to do this)
«persuadeo» (persuade) governs the dative — strikingly NOT accusative: persuasi ei (I persuaded him). Verb of influence on the dative person
«ignosco» (forgive) governs the dative: ignosce mihi (forgive me). Double construction: ignosce mihi peccatum meum (forgive me my sin) — dat of person + acc of offense
«invideo» (envy) governs the dative: invideo tibi (I envy you). The envied person takes the dative — counterintuitive for English speakers
«indulgeo» (indulge/be lenient toward) governs the dative: indulget filio (he is lenient with his son)
«irascor» (be angry at) — deponent, governs the dative: irascor tibi (I am angry at you)
«minor» (threaten) — deponent, governs the dative of person threatened + acc of threat: mihi mortem minatur (he threatens me with death)
«fido» (trust) governs dative or ablative: fidit amicis (he trusts his friends). Compare confido
«confido» (trust strongly) governs dative or ablative: confido tibi (I trust you). Slight stylistic variation between cases
«diffido» (distrust) governs the dative: diffidit sibi (he distrusts himself). Mirror of confido
«praecipio» (instruct/command) governs dative of person + acc of thing: praecipio tibi hoc (I instruct you in this)
«impero» (command) governs the dative: impero militibus (I command the soldiers). Hence imperator (commander) — one who commands those in dative position
«praesum» (be in charge of) governs the dative: praesum exercitui (I am in command of the army). Compound of prae + sum
«praeficio» (put X in charge of Y) governs acc of X + dat of Y: praefecit eum exercitui (he put him in command of the army)
«supplico» (beg/supplicate) governs the dative: supplicat deis (he supplicates the gods)
«resisto» (resist) governs the dative: resistit hostibus (he resists the enemies). Verbs of opposition take dat
«repugno» (oppose, fight against) governs the dative: repugnat consilio (he opposes the plan)
«occurro» (meet, run into) governs the dative: mihi in via occurrit (he met me on the road)
«obtempero» (obey, comply with) governs the dative: obtempero legibus (I obey the laws)
«oboedio» (obey) governs the dative: oboedit mihi (he obeys me). Source of English 'obey' with the same case logic in OF/OE
«servio» (serve, be a slave to) governs the dative: servit domino (he serves his master)
«prosum» (benefit, be useful to) governs the dative: prodest patriae (it benefits the fatherland). Compound of pro + sum
«obsum» (be a hindrance to) governs the dative: obest mihi (it harms me). Compound of ob + sum, mirror of prosum
«desum» (be lacking to) governs the dative: deest mihi pecunia (I lack money / money is lacking to me). The lacking thing in nom, the lacker in dat
«adsum» (be present to / help) governs the dative when meaning 'help': adsum tibi (I am here for you / I support you)
«memini» (remember) governs the genitive of persons and abstract things: memini patris (I remember father). Acc for concrete present things
«obliviscor» (forget) — deponent, governs the genitive: oblitus est mei (he has forgotten me). Mirror of memini
«recordor» (recall) — deponent, governs genitive or accusative: recordor temporum (I recall the times). Cor (heart) + re- = bring back to heart
«miseret» (it pities — impersonal) governs acc of person feeling pity + gen of object: miseret me tui (I pity you / it pities me of you)
«paenitet» (it repents — impersonal) governs acc of person + gen of cause: paenitet me peccati (I repent of the sin)
«pudet» (it shames — impersonal) governs acc of ashamed person + gen of cause: pudet me dicere (I am ashamed to say). One of the five impersonal feeling-verbs
«taedet» (it wearies — impersonal) governs acc of weary person + gen of cause: taedet me vitae (I am weary of life)
«piget» (it irks — impersonal) governs acc of irked person + gen of cause: piget me stultitiae meae (I am vexed by my foolishness)
«indigeo» (need, lack) governs gen or abl: indiget pecuniae (he needs money). Both cases attested
«egeo» (lack, be in need of) governs gen or abl: eget consilii (he lacks counsel). Either case is acceptable
«accuso» (accuse) governs acc of person + gen of charge: accuso eum proditionis (I accuse him of treason). Charge takes the gen
«damno» (condemn) governs acc of person + gen of charge: damnat eum capitis (he condemns him to death — of life/head)
«condemno» (condemn) governs acc of person + gen of charge: condemnat eum furti (he condemns him for theft)
«arguo» (charge with, prove) governs acc of person + gen of accusation: arguo te falsi (I accuse you of falsehood)
«absolvo» (acquit) governs acc of person + gen or abl of charge: absolvo te culpae (I absolve you of guilt). Mirror of damno
«utor» (use) — deponent, governs the ablative: utor libro (I use a book). Source of English 'use' but with ABL government — counterintuitive
«fruor» (enjoy) — deponent, governs the ablative: fruor vita (I enjoy life). Hence English 'fruit' (what one enjoys)
«fungor» (perform, discharge a duty) — deponent, governs the ablative: fungitur officio (he discharges his office)
«potior» (gain control of) — deponent, governs ablative or genitive: potitur urbe (he gains the city). Hence 'potent' (one who has gained power)
«vescor» (feed on) — deponent, governs the ablative: vescitur carne (he feeds on meat). Active form vesco does not exist in classical Latin
«careo» (lack) governs the ablative: careo amicis (I lack friends). The five 'utor-fruor-fungor-potior-vescor' deponents + careo are the canonical abl-government set
«abundo» (have in abundance, abound in) governs the ablative: abundat divitiis (he abounds in wealth)
«nitor» (rely on, lean on) — deponent, governs the ablative: nititur baculo (he leans on a staff). Also acc + inf for 'strive to'
«glorior» (boast, take pride in) — deponent, governs the ablative or de + abl: gloriatur victoriis (he boasts of his victories)
«dignor» (deem worthy of) — deponent, governs acc of person + abl of thing: dignor te honore (I deem you worthy of the honor)
«doceo» (teach) governs double accusative: doceo te grammaticam (I teach you grammar). Both person and subject in acc — distinctive Latin pattern
«celo» (conceal) governs double accusative: celo te consilium meum (I conceal my plan from you). The person concealed-from in acc — counterintuitive
«rogo» (ask) governs double accusative: rogo te sententiam (I ask you your opinion). Person and request both in acc
«peto» (seek, request) governs acc of thing + ab + abl of person: peto pacem ab eo (I seek peace from him). With acc/acc only in archaic usage
«posco» (demand) governs double accusative: posco te pecuniam (I demand money from you). Forceful version of rogo
«oro» (beg, pray) governs double accusative: oro te auxilium (I beg you for help). Source of English 'orator', 'oration'
«flagito» (demand insistently) governs double accusative: flagitat eum responsum (he demands an answer from him)
